module.title: FILTERS & SETS
module.subtitle: DISTINCT · LIMIT · IN · BETWEEN · LIKE · IS NULL
module.theory:
## Trimming the result set
A raw SELECT can dump thousands of rows. The keywords in this sector let you *narrow, slice, and pattern-match* before the data ever hits your deck.

## DISTINCT — drop the duplicates
**DISTINCT** removes repeated rows, leaving one of each unique value. Use it when a column repeats and you only want the distinct set.
~~~sql
SELECT DISTINCT district FROM runners;
~~~
**WHY:** Twelve runners might work three districts — DISTINCT collapses that to the three district names.

> WARNING — DISTINCT applies to the *whole row you SELECT*, not one column. **SELECT DISTINCT district, rep** keeps a row whenever the (district, rep) pair differs, so you can get "duplicate" districts. List only the columns you actually want unique.

## LIMIT and OFFSET — top-N and pagination
**LIMIT n** returns at most **n** rows. Pair it with **ORDER BY** to get a true top-N (highest, newest, etc.). **LIMIT n OFFSET k** skips the first **k** rows first — that is how you page through results.
~~~sql
SELECT name FROM runners ORDER BY rep DESC LIMIT 3;
SELECT name FROM runners ORDER BY rep DESC LIMIT 3 OFFSET 3;
~~~
**WHY:** The first query is the top 3 by rep; the second is "page 2" — rows 4, 5, 6.

> INTEL — LIMIT without ORDER BY gives you *some* n rows, but which ones is undefined. For a meaningful top-N, always sort first.

## IN — match a value list
**IN (a, b, c)** is shorthand for **= a OR = b OR = c**. Cleaner than chaining ORs.
~~~sql
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE district IN ('Neon Strip', 'The Sprawl');
~~~

## BETWEEN — an inclusive range
**BETWEEN low AND high** matches values from low to high, **including both ends**.
~~~sql
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE rep BETWEEN 40 AND 70;
~~~
**WHY:** That returns everyone with rep 40, 70, and everything between — both boundaries count.

> WARNING — BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends. **BETWEEN 40 AND 70** includes 40 and 70. If you meant "above 40 up to 70" you want **rep > 40 AND rep <= 70** instead.

## LIKE — pattern matching with wildcards
**LIKE** compares text against a pattern. Two wildcards: **%** matches any run of characters (including none), and **_** matches exactly one character.
~~~sql
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle LIKE 'Gh%';      -- starts with Gh
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle LIKE '%x';       -- ends with x
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle LIKE 'V_per';    -- V, one char, then per
~~~

> INTEL — SQLite's LIKE is case-insensitive for plain ASCII letters: **LIKE 'gh%'** and **LIKE 'Gh%'** match the same rows.

## IS NULL — the empty cells
NULL means "no value here" — it is **not** zero and **not** an empty string. You cannot test it with **=**. Use **IS NULL** / **IS NOT NULL**.
~~~sql
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle IS NULL;
SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle IS NOT NULL;
~~~

> WARNING — **handle = NULL** is the classic rookie mistake: it never matches anything, even blank cells. NULL is unknown, so **= NULL** is itself unknown. Always use **IS NULL**.

## The runners table
~~~text
runners(id, name, handle, district, rep, credits)
~~~
Some runners are off-grid — their **handle** is NULL.

exercises.title: KNOWN TURF
exercises.prompt: The **runners(id, name, handle, district, rep, credits)** table logs every courier in the grid. Return each **district** that has runners, with no duplicates — one **district** column. Order is not required.
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.hint: DISTINCT collapses repeated district values to one each.
exercises.lore: Three districts. Three sets of rules. None of them written down.

exercises.title: RICH LIST
exercises.prompt: Return the **name** and **credits** of the **3 richest** runners in the **runners** table, ordered by **credits** highest first.
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT name, credits FROM runners ORDER BY credits DESC;
exercises.solution: SELECT name, credits FROM runners ORDER BY credits DESC LIMIT 3;
exercises.hint: ORDER BY credits DESC, then LIMIT 3 keeps only the top three.
exercises.lore: The top of the list never stays the same for long.

exercises.title: PAGE TWO
exercises.brief: Skip the top 3, take the next 2.
exercises.prompt: Sort the **runners** by **rep** highest first, then return the **name** of runners on "page 2": **skip the first 3** and return the **next 2** names. (One **name** column, in that ranked order.)
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT name FROM runners ORDER BY rep DESC LIMIT 2;
exercises.solution: SELECT name FROM runners ORDER BY rep DESC LIMIT 2 OFFSET 3;
exercises.hint: LIMIT 2 OFFSET 3 skips the first three rows, then takes two.
exercises.lore: The mid-tier runners do the quiet work nobody headlines.

exercises.title: TWO DISTRICTS
exercises.prompt: Return the **name** of every runner working in either **Neon Strip** or **Old Town**, using an **IN** value list on **district**. Sort by **name** A→Z. (One **name** column.)
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT name FROM runners WHERE district = 'Neon Strip' ORDER BY name;
exercises.solution: SELECT name FROM runners WHERE district IN ('Neon Strip', 'Old Town') ORDER BY name;
exercises.hint: WHERE district IN ('Neon Strip', 'Old Town'), then ORDER BY name.
exercises.lore: The Sprawl keeps its own ledger. Leave it out.

exercises.title: MID-TIER SWEEP
exercises.prompt: Return the **name** and **rep** of every runner whose **rep** is **between 40 and 70 inclusive**, using **BETWEEN**. Sort by **rep** lowest first.
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT name, rep FROM runners WHERE rep > 40 AND rep < 70 ORDER BY rep;
exercises.solution: SELECT name, rep FROM runners WHERE rep BETWEEN 40 AND 70 ORDER BY rep;
exercises.hint: rep BETWEEN 40 AND 70 includes both 40 and 70.
exercises.lore: Reputation forty to seventy: trusted enough to hire, cheap enough to burn.

exercises.title: V FOR VENOM
exercises.prompt: Return the **handle** of every runner whose **handle starts with the letter V**, using **LIKE**. Sort by **handle** A→Z. (One **handle** column.)
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT handle FROM runners WHERE handle = 'V%' ORDER BY handle;
exercises.solution: SELECT handle FROM runners WHERE handle LIKE 'V%' ORDER BY handle;
exercises.hint: LIKE 'V%' — the % matches any characters after the V.
exercises.lore: Two vipers in the wire. Only one of them bites.

exercises.title: OFF THE GRID
exercises.prompt: Some runners stay anonymous — their **handle** is NULL. Return the **name** of every runner whose **handle IS NULL**. Sort by **name** A→Z. (One **name** column.)
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle = NULL ORDER BY name;
exercises.solution: SELECT name FROM runners WHERE handle IS NULL ORDER BY name;
exercises.hint: Use IS NULL, never = NULL — the equals form never matches.
exercises.lore: No handle, no trace, no obituary.

exercises.title: ON THE RECORD
exercises.brief: Top earner who has a handle.
exercises.prompt: Among runners who **have a handle** (handle IS NOT NULL), return the **name** and **credits** of the single **richest** one — highest **credits**. Return exactly one row, two columns.
exercises.setup:
CREATE TABLE runners (id INTEGER, name TEXT, handle TEXT, district TEXT, rep INTEGER, credits INTEGER);
INSERT INTO runners VALUES
(1,'Kioko','Ghost','Neon Strip',88,12000),
(2,'Reza','Viper','The Sprawl',64,5400),
(3,'Salt',NULL,'Neon Strip',41,900),
(4,'Mara','Vesper','Old Town',70,7200),
(5,'Dex','Glitch','The Sprawl',55,3300),
(6,'Wren',NULL,'Old Town',30,1500),
(7,'Juno','Onyx','Neon Strip',92,15800);
exercises.starter: SELECT name, credits FROM runners ORDER BY credits DESC;
exercises.solution: SELECT name, credits FROM runners WHERE handle IS NOT NULL ORDER BY credits DESC LIMIT 1;
exercises.hint: Filter with handle IS NOT NULL, ORDER BY credits DESC, then LIMIT 1.
exercises.lore: The richest ghost on file still left a name.
